texts.text: A B S T R A C T

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this paper, a commercial spiral wound PGMD module was modeled and optimized for seawater desalination using Response Surface Methodology (RSM). Permeate fl ux (Pflux) and speci fi c thermal energy consumption (STEC) were the main performance parameters to optimize, while evaporator inlet temperature (Tevap), condenser inlet temperature (Tcond) and feed fl ow rate (F) were the three operating parameters chosen. Analysis of variance (ANOVA) was used to evaluate statistically the response surface models. According to the study, Tevap had the strongest e ff ect on Pfl ux and STEC, increasing the former and decreasing the latter, F increased both responses, and Tcond had a weak e ff ect on Pfl ux and practically none on STEC. The models were validated with further experimental data and a good correlation between experimental and predicted values of the responses was obtained for Pflux and STEC respectively. An optimization was performed to determine the operating conditions that produce a maximum value of Pflux and a minimum value of STEC simultaneously. The result of the multiple responses optimization using desirability function was a maximum Pflux of 2.66 l/h·m 2 and a minimum STEC of 255.8 kWh/m 3 .

texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane Distillation (MD) is a thermal separation technology especially used for desalination purposes, in which only vapour molecules can pass through a porous membrane, which is hydrophobic. The liquid feed to be treated is in direct contact with the surface of the membrane, but it does not penetrate the pores due to the hydrophobic nature. The driving force of the process is the vapour pressure di ff erence across the membrane, which is usually established by a temperature di ff erence. MD presents several advantages compared to other membrane separation technologies [1 -3]. Being an evaporative process, it is not limited by the osmotic pressure (unlike reverse osmosis), thus it has an interesting potential to treat feeds with a higher concentration of solutes [4 -6]. In addition, the relatively low operating temperatures (between 60 and 85 °C) make feasible the use of renewable energy, reducing the consumption of fossil fuels and with that the greenhouse gas emissions [7,8]. Generally, the feed does not require a pretreatment and the quality of the permeate production is practically independent of the concentration of the feed, with a rejection of nonvolatile solutes of theoretically 100% [9]. texts.orig: The high energy consumption coupled to the lack of membranes designed and manufactured speci fi cally for MD [26] are setbacks for the commercial implementation of the technology. Although commercial modules have been developed, the majority of the operational experience in MD has been carried out at bench scale and only few studies with commercial modules are available. Air gap membrane distillation modules have been studied both in plate and frame [27] and spiralwound con fi guration [28,29]. Permeate gap modules have also been analyzed at pilot-scale, both in plate and frame [30] and spiral-wound con fi guration [31,32]. Comparisons of commercial modules of both air gap and permeate gap have also been published [33,34]. However, none of these works dealt with the determination of the optimum operating conditions for commercial modules in pilot applications.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Characterization of the performance of membrane distillation has been mostly carried out using conventional method of experimentation, changing one of the operating variables while keeping the others constant. Conventional methods involve a large number of experimental runs, which ignore the interactions between the dominant variables a ff ecting the performance of the process and require much time. The most important properties for evaluating the performance of MD are the productivity and the thermal energy e ffi ciency. The former is characterized by the permeate fl ux (Pflux), while the latter can be studied using di ff erent parameters. The most common are the speci fi c thermal energy consumption (STEC) and the gained output ratio (GOR), which is the ratio between the latent heat required to vaporize the total permeate production and the total amount of heat supplied [35]. Response Surface Methodology is a statistical method used for fi nding the relationship between one or more responses and input variables or factors. RSM is also used to minimize the number of experimental tests and provide multiple regression approach to optimize the responses. It has been applied to MD, but most researchers have focused on the study of the permeate production in MD and its optimization, leaving aside the thermodynamic study [36 -40]. Mass transfer is in fl uenced by the heat transfer, and there is a trade-o ff between Pflux and energy e ffi ciency in MD which is maximized for commercial size modules [34]. This poses a con fl ict that a ff ects the maximization of the total e ffi ciency of the process. The few works that have dealt with the optimization of both performance parameters (permeate production and energy consumption), treated each one separately without optimizing both at the same time [41,42]. In this work a complete characterization of a commercial MD module manufactured by Solar Spring has been done in a pilot plant, considering the e ff ect of the main operating conditions in productivity and energy consumption to later fi nd the optimum conditions that maximize permeate production and simultaneously minimize energy consumption.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The Oryx 150 is a spiral wound MD system commercialized by Solar Spring (Fig. 1). Solar Spring company, in collaboration with Fraunhofer ISE, has developed spiral wound MD modules with very e ffi cient internal heat recovery [43]. The system was integrated in a pilot plant located at Plataforma Solar de Almería (SE Spain) which is powered by solar thermal energy. The operation of the Oryx 150 was as follows (Fig. 2): the feed water was pumped from a feed tank, after circulating in the condenser channel, the pre-heated feed was additionally heated up in a heat exchanger connected to the solar fi eld and then it fl owed along the evaporator channel, ending up into the feed tank for recirculation. The permeate was collected in a permeate tank for measuring its mass with a balance and then it was returned to the feed tank.

texts.orig: The MD module of the Oryx 150 system has a PGMD con fi guration. The inlets and the outlets of the condenser and evaporator channel are located at the top of the module. In the wound, the evaporator channel faces the inside of the module, whereas the condenser channel is placed toward the shell of the module to minimize the heat losses. The module has a channel length of 7 m, channel height of 0.7 m, channel width of 3.2 mm and gap width of 1 mm. The membrane is made of PTFE with an area of 10 m 2 , a pore size of 0.05 μ m, a thickness of 70 μ m and a porosity of 80%.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The heat source was a solar thermal fi eld of Flat-Plate Collectors (Solaris CP1 Nova by Solaris, Spain) divided into two fi les, each one consisting of fi ve collectors. The nominal thermal power of this solar fi eld was approximately 7 kWth for a temperature of 90 °C. The evaporator inlet temperature was controlled by regulating the heat supply from the solar fi eld to the heat exchanger. This was achieved by adjusting the fl ow rate of the hot fl uid circulating through the solar fi eld, as well as its temperature, which was controlled with the help of an air cooler coupled to the collectors. The maximum accepted deviation for the evaporator inlet temperature was ± 1 °C. A centrifugal stainless steel pump (WILO-Stratos 25/1-6 CAN PN 10) was used in the solar fi eld. All components in contact with the water were made of corrosionresistant materials. Four glass-encapsulated Pt100 temperature sensors were positioned directly at the condenser inlet, condenser outlet, evaporator inlet and evaporator outlet of the MD module. The volumetric feed fl ow rate was measured by a visual fl ow meter at the condenser inlet position and adjusted manually. The hydraulic pressure at the condenser inlet was evaluated relative to the ambient with a WIKA pressure sensor. In the solar fi eld, resistance thermometers with standard head IP67 equipped with thermowell, electromagnetic fl ow meters (Promag 50P15 by Endress + Hauser) and pressure transmitters (S10 absolute by WIKA) were employed. The global solar radiation was measured using a pyranometer (CM6B by Kipp + Zonen). All measurements were monitored and controlled by a Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition system connected through a Programmable Logic Controller.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In order to carry out the 16 experiments required by the face-centered CCD design, synthetic seawater was prepared at a concentration of 3.5 wt%, using raw sea salts from the saltworks of Cabo de Gata, Almería, Spain. Each experiment was performed in stationary conditions, keeping constant the input factors, namely, Tevap, Tcond and F. To guarantee steady-state operation, each experiment was run for 75 min discarding the fi rst 15 min. After the fi rst 15 min, 4 measurements of permeate fl ow rate were taken manually during 60 min, spaced every 15 min. So, the values represent averages during 15 min intervals.

texts.orig: Scho fi eld et al. [51] predicted the e ff ect of temperature polarization on mass fl ux establishing that if fl ux appears linear with the membrane temperature and the di ff erence in the fl ux is only 3-fold for an increase of membrane temperature from 30 °C to 80 °C, it is clear that temperature polarization e ff ect is excessive (temperature polarization coe ffi cient close to 0). However, if the fl ux increases exponentially by 5fold considering the same range of temperature, it indicates that the temperature polarization e ff ect is low (temperature polarization coeffi cient near to 1). In the commercial modules there is another e ff ect that dominates over the temperature polarization: the decrease of the driving force of the process due to the length of the channel. Most of the works shown in the literature are for modules with small lengths (0.215 m [47]; 0.1 m [52]; 0.055 m [50]). However, Oryx 150 had a length of 7 m. The greater length of the channel in commercial modules, increases the residence time. Consequently, the driving force in commercial modules is lower (Fig. 7) and so the vapour pressure differences can be approximated to linear according to the Antoine equation. Other researchers have also reported a linear growth of Pflux with Tevap in large-size modules [27,53]. The lower driving force in commercial modules has also been reported before [29,54].

texts.orig: Higher F also produces higher Pflux, but the variation is lower than with Tevap. The maximum increase reached is 0.6-fold when F rises from 400 l/h to 600 l/h. For a higher F, the thermal boundary layer gets thinner, increasing the heat transfer coe ffi cient and therefore reducing the temperature polarization e ff ect. This means that the temperature at the membrane surface is closer to the bulk temperature enhancing the driving force and therefore increasing the vapour fl ux. Finally, Tcond has an inverse relationship with the Pflux. At low Tcond, a larger vapour pressure di ff erence is reached resulting in higher Pfl ux. The results indicate that the e ff ect of Tcond in Pfl ux is lower than the e ff ect of Tevap, namely a maximum 0.3-fold decrease for Tcond changing from 20 °C to 30 °C (Figs. 6c -f). This is due to the exponential relationship between vapour pressure and temperature. The variation of vapour pressure is more pronounced at high level of temperatures than at low temperatures, as can be seen in Fig. 8, so an increase in Tevap has more in fl uence than an increase in Tcond for the same bulk temperature di ff erence

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The e ff ect of the operating parameters (Tevap, Tcond and F) on STEC is shown in Fig. 8a -f. It can be observed that operating at a high Tevap increases the thermal e ffi ciency. In general the increase of Tevap from 60 °C to 80 °C while keeping constant Tcond and F results in a decrease of STEC of 0.3-fold. When high Tevap is applied, more thermal energy is required but since the temperature di ff erence between the evaporator and condenser channel is higher and this enhances the driving force (Fig. 7), the resulting increase of Pfl ux compensates the higher thermal energy required, reducing the STEC. Conversely, an increase of the feed fl ow rate from 400 l/h to 600 l/h leads to an increase of STEC of approximately 0.1-fold. As discussed previously, a higher F leads to a higher Pfl ux, however, in this case this does not compensate the decrease of heat recovery due to the lower residence time of the feed inside the condenser channel, enhancing the external heat required. The e ff ect of Tcond is practically negligible on STEC, being lower than 0.1-fold. In principle, the value of the condenser inlet temperature could be important because a higher Tcond decreases the external heat required for the process. However, if Tcond is larger, the driving force is lower for the same value of Tevap, and the decrease in production cancels the e ff ect of the reduction of heat input. This is an interesting result, since requirements of external cooling can be avoided when the source water for desalination is at 30 °C or less.

texts.orig: It can be seen that the latent heat that passes through the membrane increases with Tevap, as expected due to the higher amount of vapour that crosses the membrane. As Tevap increases, the latent heat grows up until fi nally surpassing the sensible heat losses by conduction. Thus, working at high temperatures means that more of the input heat is used to produce permeate and is not turned into sensible heat. Both, latent heat and sensible heat transfer increase with F. At a larger feed fl ow rate, the permeate production is greater, increasing the latent heat that passes through the membrane. Moreover, the higher F increases the heat transfer coe ffi cient and thus the sensible heat losses are larger.

texts.orig: 3.3. Optimization of multiple responses: Pflux and STEC
texts.text: 3.3. Optimization of multiple responses: Pflux and STEC

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The Derringer function, or desirability function, is the most important and the most used in the multi-criteria methodology for optimization [55]. This methodology is based on the construction of a desirable function for each individual response. The scale of the desirability oscillates between d = 0 for a completely undesirable response and d = 1 for a completely desirable response. The overall desirability is de fi ned as the weighted geometric mean of the individual desirability of each response. In this study, it is necessary to perform the optimization for two cases, namely, the maximization of Pflux and the minimization of STEC, using the corresponding simpli fi ed response surface correlations as objective functions. The combination of factor levels which maximized the function desirability for Pflux was 79.9 °C for Tevap, 20.1 °C for Tcond, and 589.7 l/h for F, and the optimum levels of the factors to minimize STEC were 80 °C for Tevap, and 400 l/h for F. The obtained desirability values were 1 for the maximization of Pflux and 0.988 for the minimization of STEC. The values of Pflux and STEC when the objective was the maximization of Pflux, were 2.68 l/h·m 2 and 257.4 kWh/m 3 . The minimization of STEC was achieved with Tevap value similar to the previous case, but F was much lower. While in the fi rst case F was close to the maximum value (589.7 l/h), in the optimization of STEC, F was the lowest (400 l/h). As shown before, Tcond did not in fl uence the value of STEC, so its value was not determining. By applying the optimum conditions obtained in this latter case, the value of STEC was minimum and equal to 225.79 kWh/m 3 , and Pflux was 1.97 l/h·m 2 .
texts.text: The Derringer function, or desirability function, is the most important and the most used in the multi-criteria methodology for optimization [55]. This methodology is based on the construction of a desirable function for each individual response. The scale of the desirability oscillates between d = 0 for a completely undesirable response and d = 1 for a completely desirable response. The overall desirability is de fi ned as the weighted geometric mean of the individual desirability of each response. In this study, it is necessary to perform the optimization for two cases, namely, the maximization of Pflux and the minimization of STEC, using the corresponding simpli fi ed response surface correlations as objective functions. The combination of factor levels which maximized the function desirability for Pflux was 79.9 °C for Tevap, 20.1 °C for Tcond, and 589.7 l/h for F, and the optimum levels of the factors to minimize STEC were 80 °C for Tevap, and 400 l/h for F. The obtained desirability values were 1 for the maximization of Pflux and 0.988 for the minimization of STEC. The values of Pflux and STEC when the objective was the maximization of Pflux, were 2.68 l/h·m 2 and 257.4 kWh/m 3 . The minimization of STEC was achieved with Tevap value similar to the previous case, but F was much lower. While in the fi rst case F was close to the maximum value (589.7 l/h), in the optimization of STEC, F was the lowest (400 l/h). As shown before, Tcond did not in fl uence the value of STEC, so its value was not determining. By applying the optimum conditions obtained in this latter case, the value of STEC was minimum and equal to 225.79 kWh/m 3 , and Pflux was 1.97 l/h·m 2 .

However, the desirability function can be used to optimize several responses at the same time and to fi nd optimal compromises between the di ff erent responses considered. texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusions
texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Response surface methodology (RSM) was proved to be a satisfactory tool to develop a model for a commercial spiral-wound PGMD module to desalinate a solution of marine salts at 3.5 wt%. Also, it was used to fi nd the optimum operating conditions that maximize the productivity and the thermal energy e ffi ciency. The obtained response surface models predicted the permeate fl ux and the speci fi c thermal energy e ffi ciency as a function of the main operating variables, obtaining a maximum deviation between measured and predicted results of 7% for the former and 12% for the latter. From the analysis, it can be concluded that the evaporator inlet temperature had the strongest e ff ect on permeate fl ux and speci fi c thermal energy consumption, both increasing with Tevap. Feed fl ow rate had a lesser e ff ect, more important on permeate fl ux than on speci fi c thermal energy consumption. Condenser inlet temperature only had an in fl uence on permeate fl ux. Therefore, if thermal energy e ffi ciency is to be optimized in the operation of this commercial module, external cooling is not required when the source water for desalination (which is used in the condensing channel) is at 30 °C or less.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Individual optimization of each performance response was shown to be con fl icting due to contrary in fl uences from the operating variables. However, multiple optimization succeeded in fi nding values of the operating variables that simultaneously maximize permeate fl ux and minimize speci fi c thermal energy consumption. Under evaporator inlet temperature of 80 °C, condenser inlet temperature of 20 °C and feed fl ow rate of 582.7 l/h the maximum permeate fl ux obtained was 2.66 l/ h·m 2 and the minimum speci fi c thermal energy consumption was 255.8 kWh/m 3 .
